chunk_summary: This section discusses integrating clinic counseling with India's ICDS, ASHA, POSHAN, and NRC networks to improve infant and young child feeding practices, emphasizing program touchpoints, referral pathways, and community-based interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of ICDS, ASHA, and POSHAN in supporting infant and young child feeding (IYCF).
Learn how to document and track feeding status using core IYCF indicators and child health cards.
Identify referral pathways for malnourished children and coordinate follow-up care through NRCs and community workers.
Explore culturally effective counseling strategies for low-literacy families and working mothers.
key_facts: Core IYCF indicators include early initiation, exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) under 6 months, timely complementary feeding at 6 months, minimum meal frequency (MMF), minimum dietary diversity (MDD), and minimum acceptable diet (MAD).
Anganwadi workers under ICDS provide growth monitoring and take-home rations for children aged 6–36 months.
ASHA workers reinforce breastfeeding, complementary feeding, and iron adherence during HBNC and HBYC home visits.
Digital tools like POSHAN Tracker support growth data continuity and early escalation for weight faltering.
Children with wasting, edema, or persistent feeding refusal should be referred to Nutrition Rehabilitation Centers (NRCs).
Vitamin A supplementation schedules are tracked via child health cards and delivered through Anganwadi outreach sessions.
reference_quote: Under ICDS, Anganwadi workers provide growth monitoring and take-home rations for 6–36 months; counsel caregivers on correct preparation so rations are not diluted into low-calorie gruels. (Reference: Ministry of Women and Child Development India, Integrated Child Development Services Scheme)
document_key_topics: Infant and young child feeding indicators and tracking
